questions.question: Why is deterministic hash-based assignment preferred over database-based assignment in the hot path?
options: Hash assignment requires no database lookup — it computes the same bucket from user_id + experiment_id alone, enabling sub-millisecond assignment at any scale without adding a DB dependency to every page load.
Database assignment is less accurate because databases can have consistency issues.
Hash assignment avoids the need to store any experiment configuration.
Database-based assignment requires a separate data center for each experiment.
questions.explanation: The assignment service is in the critical path of every page load. Adding a database call would add latency and a new failure mode. Deterministic hashing trades dynamic reassignment flexibility for zero-latency, zero-dependency assignment.

questions.question: What is the peeking problem in A/B testing?
options: Declaring statistical significance prematurely by checking results before the experiment has run long enough, which inflates false positive rates because the significance boundary is crossed by random fluctuation.
A bug where subscribers can see their treatment assignment in the UI.
A security issue where experiment parameters are visible in network traffic.
An encoding problem where different treatments receive different video quality.
questions.explanation: p-values are calibrated for a single check at the end of the experiment. Checking repeatedly inflates the type I error rate. The fix is sequential testing or minimum run duration enforcement.

questions.question: How does including experiment_id in the hash key guarantee independent assignments across experiments?
options: Different experiment_ids produce different hash values for the same user_id, so a subscriber's bucket in experiment A is independent of their bucket in experiment B.
Experiment IDs are allocated from a separate namespace that prevents bucket collisions.
The hash function uses experiment_id as a salt that is only known to the assignment service.
Including experiment_id ensures all subscribers get the same treatment across experiments.
questions.explanation: hash(user_id + experiment_A_id) and hash(user_id + experiment_B_id) are independent. The same subscriber may be in control for A and treatment for B — exactly the desired property.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a global holdout group?
options: To measure the cumulative long-term effect of all features shipped over a period, by comparing a permanently unexposed group to the rest of the subscriber base.
To provide a control group for each individual A/B experiment.
To exclude subscribers who have opted out of personalization.
To reduce the load on the assignment service by routing some subscribers away from experiments.
questions.explanation: Individual experiments measure incremental effects. The holdout group measures aggregate drift: is the product actually improving over 6 months when accounting for all shipped features combined?

questions.question: Why must experiments on the same UI surface be in a mutual exclusion group?
options: If two UI experiments overlap on the same subscriber, it is impossible to attribute a metric change to either experiment — the effects are confounded and both results are invalid.
Mutual exclusion is required to stay within the 10,000-bucket limit of the assignment service.
Overlapping UI experiments cause rendering errors on the subscriber's device.
Mutual exclusion reduces the compute cost of the assignment service.
questions.explanation: Contaminated experiments produce uninterpretable results. Mutual exclusion ensures clean attribution.

questions.question: What is the correct fallback behavior when the assignment service is temporarily unavailable?
options: Serve the control treatment to all affected subscribers — never fail a page load because of an assignment service outage.
Block the page load until assignment service recovers.
Randomly assign subscribers to treatments without recording the assignment.
Route all traffic to the treatment group during the outage.
questions.explanation: The assignment service must never be in the critical failure path. Defaulting to control is safe, auditable, and preserves the subscriber experience.

questions.question: Which challenge is unique to running 1,000+ simultaneous experiments at Netflix scale?
options: A subscriber may be in dozens of experiments simultaneously — the assignment service must resolve all active experiments per request in under 5ms without any external calls.
More than 100 experiments require a distributed database that cannot fit on one server.
Statistical significance thresholds must be manually adjusted for each experiment.
The Kafka metrics pipeline becomes a bottleneck when more than 500 experiments are active.
questions.explanation: At 1,000+ experiments, the assignment service must load all experiment configs in-memory and compute bucket assignments for all of them in a single sub-5ms operation. This requires compact in-memory representation and efficient hashing.
